title: Competing incommensurability, electronic correlations, and superconductivity in a hybrid transition metal dichalcogenide
authors: Jean C. Souza, Moshe Haim, Lorenzo Crippa, Hyeonhu Bae, Edanel Fishbein, Jonathan Ruhman, Binghai Yan, Amit Kanigel, Roser Valentí, Nurit Avraham, Haim Beidenkopf
abstract: The engineering of superlattices in two-dimensional van der Waals materials has enabled the realization of rich phase diagrams hosting topological and strongly correlated phases. While incommensurability is widespread in three-dimensional systems, the role of moiré potentials in bulk materials remains largely unexplored. Here, using scanning tunneling microscopy, we demonstrate that a bulk transition-metal dichalcogenide polytype, 4Hb-TaS$_2$, hosts an emergent incommensurate potential between its alternating 1T and 1H layers. Interplay with a concomitant incommensurate charge-density wave suppresses the long-range order of this potential, leading to intricate coupling with electronic correlations in the doped 1T surface layer. Combining density functional theory with dynamical mean-field theory, we show that the lattice mismatch locally modulates the interlayer distance, thereby tuning both hybridization and charge transfer between the correlated 1T and metallic 1H layers. This redistribution of charge drives the system towards a doped Mott regime, in which the remaining local moments become self-screened, giving rise to a zero-bias resonance. We further find that bulk superconductivity competes with both the underlying landscape and the associated charge transfer. Our results establish incommensurate potentials as a previously overlooked ingredient in hybrid transition-metal dichalcogenides, highlighting their central role in the interplay between electronic correlations, charge-density-wave order, and unconventional superconductivity.
summary: This paper identifies that an intrinsic incommensurate potential in 4Hb-TaS2 regulates electronic correlations and charge transfer between its 1T and 1H layers. This modulation creates a doped Mott regime and establishes a complex competition between the incommensurate landscape, charge-density waves, and superconductivity. The findings highlight the importance of bulk incommensurability in tuning many-body physics in hybrid materials.
main_problem: The study aims to understand the origin of the electronic structure and the zero-bias conductance peak in the hybrid transition metal dichalcogenide 4Hb-TaS2, specifically investigating how incommensurate potentials and charge transfer influence electronic correlations and superconductivity.
main_result: The researchers discovered that an intrinsic incommensurate potential, caused by a 1.1% lattice mismatch between 1T and 1H layers, modulates interlayer hybridization and charge transfer, driving the system into a doped Mott regime where superconductivity competes with the incommensurate landscape.
method: The study combines experimental Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (STM) and Spectroscopy (STS) with computational Density Functional Theory (DFT) and Dynamical Mean-Field Theory (DMFT) using a periodic Anderson model.
model_or_system: The physical system is the bulk transition metal dichalcogenide polytype 4Hb-TaS2, consisting of alternating correlated 1T and metallic 1H layers. The theoretical model is a periodic Anderson model accounting for correlated d-orbitals and uncorrelated c-electrons.
key_observables: Zero-bias conductance peak (ZBCP), Upper and Lower Hubbard Bands (UHB/LHB), charge transfer (CT) magnitude, and spectral weight at the Fermi energy.
important_parameters: Lattice mismatch (~1.1%), Hubbard interaction (U = 100 meV), superconducting transition temperature (Tc approx 2.6 K), and Selenium doping concentration (x = 0% to 5%).
main_assumptions: The computational approach uses a local-registry approximation for the incommensurate potential rather than a full moire supercell, and DMFT is applied to a simplified periodic Anderson model.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the crystal structure and STM topography; Fig 2 illustrates the modulation of charge transfer and hybridization by lattice mismatch; Fig 3 shows DMFT calculations of ZBCP and Hubbard band evolution; Fig 4 demonstrates the effect of superconductivity on CDW configuration transitions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the hybrid TMD system, presents STM/STS evidence for an intrinsic incommensurate potential, uses DFT/DMFT to model the charge transfer and many-body effects, and concludes by discussing the competition between this potential and superconductivity.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 Intrinsic origin for undepleted CDW sites in 4Hb-TaS2. a Crystal structure of 4Hb- TaS2. b Representative topography taken at T = 4.2 K for xSe = 1%. c Representative dI/dV spectra of occupied and depleted CDW sites (red and blue lines, respectively) taken at T = 4.2 K for xSe = 1% (Vbias = 200 mV, Isp = 200 pA, VAC = 5 meV). d, e Representative topographies for xSe = 2%, 0%. All topographies were taken with Vbias = 100 mV and Isp = 50 pA, and the white scale bar is 20 nm. f Percentage of the total sites that are occupied as a function of Se doping, xSe. The statistical analysis is further described in the SM. The solid purple line is a linear fit.

Fig. 2 Modulation of the charge transfer and the hybridization strength by the lattice mismatch. a Clustered averages of four different types of spectra obtained at the same 1T surface (Vbias = 120 mV, Isp = 150 pA, VAC = 5 mV). The shaded margins represent the standard deviation for each spectrum. b DFT calculated DOS for the 1T layer of bilayer 1T/1H-TaS2 in selected lateral displacements (marked in c). The labels denote the experimental spectral archetypes; in the DFT calculations, they identify representative local registries with different residual fillings of the 1T- derived flat band. c Relative energy landscape ∆E for lateral displacement of a 1T layer on a fixed 1H layer. Energies...

Fig. 3 The origin of the screening metal. a DMFT calculated spectra on the 4 (meta) stable moir´e positions identified in DFT (marked by corresponding symbols in Fig. 2c). b Comparison between the ZBCP width for xSe = 0% and 1%. Around 400 occupied sites spectra were analyzed for xSe = 0% and about 600 sites for xSe = 1%. The inset shows representative ZBCP spectra from both sample types. c Asymmetry of the LHB and the UHB energy relative to EF for xSe = 1%. About 250 occupied spectra were analyzed.

Fig. 4 The effect of superconductivity on the breathing transition of the incommensu- rate potential. a Change of the enumerated ‘occupation level’ of the CDW sites ∆nocc between T = 4.2 K to T = 1.1 K for sample S1. Change of the ‘occupation level’, ∆nocc, for b µ0H = 0 T, c µ0H∥c = 1.7 T and d µ0H⊥c = 1.7 T in sample S2. The measurements were first taken at T = 4.2 K with the respective applied magnetic fields, then the sample was cooled down with the applied field for the measurements at T = 1.1 K. All samples had xSe = 0.
